blocks.statement: I can compute the heat needed to phase-change a mass using Q = mL, explain why temperature is constant during phase changes, and add multiple Q's across a full heating curve (ice → water → steam).

blocks.whatWeKnow: 2026-XJ doesn't just heat up at impact — it MELTS rock, then VAPORIZES rock. Each transition takes enormous energy.
blocks.connection: The crater's heat doesn't just raise rock temperature; it phases rock into vapor. Day 11 uses latent heat values to compute the vaporization energy at the impact site.

blocks.markdown:
## When the thermometer stops moving

Heat a block of ice and watch the thermometer. It climbs… then STOPS at 0 °C while the ice melts… then climbs again… then STOPS at 100 °C while the water boils. During the flat stretches you're still pouring energy in — it's just going into **breaking molecular bonds**, not into faster molecules.

The energy price of a phase change is:

**Q = m · L**

where L is the **latent heat** in J/kg. For water:

| Transition | L (J/kg) |
|---|---|
| Melting / freezing (L_f, fusion) | 334,000 |
| Boiling / condensing (L_v, vaporization) | 2,260,000 |

Notice L_v is about **7× bigger** than L_f — fully separating molecules into a gas costs far more than loosening them into a liquid.

A full heating curve (say, ice at −10 °C → steam at 110 °C) is just an **addition problem**: sloped segments use Q = mcΔT (with the right c for ice, water, or steam), flat segments use Q = mL. Compute each piece, add them up.

terms.definition: A transition between solid ⇄ liquid ⇄ gas. Temperature stays CONSTANT during the change while energy goes into breaking molecular bonds (not into KE). Example: pure ice at 0 °C absorbs heat → stays at 0 °C while it melts → only AFTER it's all liquid does the temperature rise.
terms.cognate: Sp. cambio de fase · Pt. mudança de fase · HC chanjman faz

terms.definition: Energy per kg required for a phase change at the transition temperature. L_f = latent heat of FUSION (melt/freeze). L_v = latent heat of VAPORIZATION (boil/condense). Units: J/kg. Water L_f = 334,000 J/kg — to melt 1 kg of ice: Q = 1 · 334,000 = 334 kJ. Water L_v = 2,260,000 J/kg — boiling needs 7× more energy than melting! 'Latent' means hidden — the energy goes into breaking bonds, not into temperature.
terms.cognate: Sp. calor latente · Pt. calor latente · HC chalè latant

blocks.title: Adding heat but the thermometer doesn't move?
blocks.markdown: During a phase change you ARE adding energy — but it's breaking bonds, not raising the average molecular KE. So T holds constant until the entire mass has changed phase. A thermometer reading a flat line does NOT mean the heat stopped.

blocks.title: Heating curve — 0.5 kg of water from ice at −10 °C to steam at 110 °C
blocks.genPrompt: Heating curve for 0.5 kg: warm ice (−10→0 °C, 10 kJ), melt flat at 0 °C (167 kJ), warm water (0→100 °C, 209 kJ), boil flat at 100 °C (1130 kJ), warm steam (100→110 °C, 10 kJ). The boiling plateau dominates the x-axis.

blocks.instruction: Label each segment of the heating curve with its equation (Q = mcΔT or Q = mL). Mark where T stays constant. Which flat segment is LONGER — melting or boiling? Why?
prompts: Copy the staircase shape; label all 5 segments with the right equation.
One sentence: why is the boiling plateau so much longer?

blocks.prompt: How much heat is needed to melt 0.5 kg of ice at 0 °C? (L_f = 334 kJ/kg.)
blocks.answer: 167 kJ — and the ice stays at 0 °C the whole time.

blocks.prompt: Total heat to convert 0.5 kg of ice at -10 °C to steam at 110 °C? Five sub-steps. Add them up.
blocks.equation: Q_total = Q_warm_ice + Q_melt + Q_warm_water + Q_boil + Q_warm_steam
blocks.answer: ≈ 1.5 × 10⁶ J. The BOILING step alone is ~75% of the total energy.

blocks.prompt: How much heat is needed to vaporize 0.2 kg of water already at 100 °C? (L_v = 2,260,000 J/kg.)
blocks.givenHint: m = 0.2 kg · L_v = 2,260,000 J/kg · water is ALREADY at 100 °C, so no warming step.
blocks.equationHint: Q = m · L_v — one step, no ΔT term (the temperature doesn't change during the phase change).

blocks.title: Why sweating works
blocks.markdown: This is why sweating cools you: every drop of sweat that evaporates carries off 2,260 kJ/kg of body heat. The high L_v of water is a survival adaptation.

blocks.prompt: How much heat to vaporize 0.2 kg of water at 100 °C? (Use L_v = 2,260,000 J/kg.) Then complete the frame.
blocks.frame: During a phase change, temperature stays ___ because energy is going into ___, not into ___. Boiling water requires ___× more energy than melting the same mass of ice.

blocks.title: Read & practice
blocks.markdown: Open the reader and read **23.1–23.8** (evaporation, condensation, boiling, freezing, regelation, and the energy of phase changes). Answer the questions beside the reading as you go.
